summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on Electronic Arts' early days when the company positioned computer programmers as 'software artists' and creative visionaries, contrasting this with EA's current status as a corporate franchise machine. He recalls being inspired as a teenager by EA's 'We See Farther' ad campaign, which suggested programming could be cool and artistic. The post highlights EA's innovative origins, including creating the sports game franchise genre with Dr. J and Larry Bird Go One on One. Atwood quotes extensively from EA's original manifesto about nurturing independent software artists, then notes with some melancholy that this idealistic vision proved difficult to sustain.
key_points: EA's 'We See Farther' ad campaign in the 1980s portrayed programmers as artists and creative visionaries, not just technical workers
Early EA games were packaged to look like rock album covers, reinforcing the artistic identity
EA invented the sports game franchise genre with Dr. J and Larry Bird Go One on One
EA's original manifesto promised a supportive environment for 'talented, independent software artists' with room for big ideas to grow
The contrast between EA's artistic origins and its current corporate franchise model illustrates how difficult it is to maintain creative vision at scale
money_quotes: I wondered, could being a computer programmer be... cool?
According to Electronic Arts, computer programmers weren't just programmers; they could be software artists.
We learn, for instance, that we are more entertained by involvement of our imaginations than by passive viewing and listening.
We're providing a special environment for talented, independent software artists. It's a supportive environment, in which big ideas are given room to grow.

We think our current work reflects this very special commitment. And though we are few in number today and apart from the mainstream of the mass software marketplace, we are confident that both time and vision are on our side.
I sure wanted to believe in software artistry at the time. EA's history proves that this is an unusually difficult vision statement to realize.
key_insight: The idealistic vision of programmers as creative artists, once championed by early EA, has proven nearly impossible to sustain as companies scale into corporate franchises.
